documents.content: The 12 behavioral questions that come up in 80% of interviews — prepare a story for each: (1) Tell me about yourself / walk me through your background. (2) Tell me about a challenging project you worked on. (3) Describe a time you disagreed with a teammate or manager. (4) Tell me about your biggest professional failure and what you learned. (5) Describe a time you had to learn something quickly. (6) Tell me about a time you went above and beyond. (7) Describe a project that didn't go as planned and how you handled it. (8) Tell me about a time you had to influence without authority. (9) Describe a difficult technical decision you made. (10) Tell me about a time you received tough feedback. (11) Why do you want to work here? (12) Why are you leaving your current role / why are you looking?

How to answer 'Tell me about yourself' — the most common opener and the one most candidates fluff: aim for 90 seconds, structured as Past-Present-Future. Past: 1-2 sentences on your background (where you studied, early career). Present: 30-45 seconds on your current role, what you're working on, and ONE notable accomplishment with metrics. Future: 1-2 sentences on why you're looking and what excites you about this role. Avoid: listing every job title you've held, going chronologically through resume, mentioning personal hobbies (unless asked), spending 4 minutes on context. Practice this until it's exactly 90 seconds, conversational, and ends with a hook the interviewer wants to follow up on.

How to answer 'Why do you want to work here?' — interviewers immediately filter out candidates who clearly haven't researched the company. Show three things: (1) Knowledge of the company's product or mission (mention something specific, not a generic 'I admire your innovation'). (2) Connection to your interests or career goals — why does THIS company fit your trajectory, vs the dozens of others hiring. (3) Excitement about the specific team or role if you know anything about it (this often comes from research on the hiring manager's LinkedIn or recent engineering blog posts). A weak answer: 'You're a great company and I want to grow.' A strong answer: 'I've been following your work on X — specifically the blog post your CTO wrote about Y — and the problem space resonates with what I built at my last company. The role on team Z would let me go deeper on this.'

How to answer 'Tell me about a conflict with a coworker': pick a real conflict but one with a constructive resolution. The interviewer is testing whether you can disagree without destroying relationships and whether you can take perspective. Structure: (1) Brief situation — what was the disagreement, who was involved. (2) Acknowledge the other person's perspective — show you tried to understand their reasoning. (3) Explain how you advocated for your position with data, not emotion. (4) Resolution — what was the outcome, ideally a compromise or one party being convinced by evidence. (5) Reflection — what you learned about handling disagreement. Avoid: portraying the other person as incompetent or malicious, framing yourself as completely right and them completely wrong, picking a conflict with someone junior to you (looks like a power play).
